title: Loop Control: break and continue
subtitle: Take precise control of your loops mid-execution
learning_objectives: Use break to immediately exit a loop when a condition is met
Use continue to skip the rest of the current iteration and proceed to the next
Use pass as a placeholder when a block is required but no action is needed

sections.heading: break and continue: Steering Mid-Loop
sections.content:
break immediately exits the entire loop — no more iterations run. It is often used to stop searching once a result has been found.

continue skips only the rest of the current iteration and jumps straight to the next one. The loop itself keeps going. Use it to skip items that don't meet a condition without nesting all your logic inside an if block.

sections.heading: pass: The Do-Nothing Placeholder
sections.content:
pass does absolutely nothing — it is a valid Python statement that simply takes up space. It is useful as a placeholder when a block is syntactically required but you haven't written the code yet, or when you intentionally want to handle a case by doing nothing.

You'll also see pass in empty function and class definitions during early development.

exercises.title: Search and Stop
exercises.prompt: Use a for loop with range(1, 25) to find the first number divisible by both 3 and 7. Print "Found: " followed by that number, then break out of the loop. Note: the answer is 21 (3×7=21), so make sure your range goes past 21.
hints: Use the modulo operator %: n % 3 == 0 means n is divisible by 3
Put break right after the print statement inside the if block

tests.message: The first number divisible by both 3 and 7 is 21 — but wait, 21 > 20! Try range(1, 25) if needed. The answer is 21.

exercises.title: Skip the Vowels
exercises.prompt: Loop over the string "programming". Use continue to skip any vowels (a, e, i, o, u) and print only the consonants, each on its own line.
exercises.starter_code:
word = "programming"
vowels = "aeiou"

for char in word:
    # Use continue to skip vowels
    pass  # replace this
hints: Use 'if char in vowels:' to check whether the character is a vowel
After the continue, any code below it in the loop body is skipped for that iteration
The print should be outside (after) the if block, not inside it

quiz.question: What is the difference between break and continue?
options: break skips one iteration; continue exits the entire loop
break exits the entire loop; continue skips to the next iteration
They are identical — both exit the loop
break only works in while loops; continue only works in for loops
quiz.explanation: break exits the entire loop immediately — no more iterations run. continue only skips the rest of the current iteration and moves on to the next one. The loop itself keeps going with continue.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of pass?
options: It skips the current loop iteration
It does nothing and acts as a placeholder where a statement is required
It passes a value to the next iteration
quiz.explanation: pass is a no-op statement. Python requires at least one statement in every indented block (like an if, for, or function body). pass satisfies that requirement without doing anything, making it perfect as a placeholder.

completion_message: Congratulations — you've completed Module 3: Control Flow! You can now write if/elif/else chains, use logical operators, loop with while and for, harness range(), and control loops precisely with break, continue, and pass. You're ready for Module 4: Functions!
